text: I would add Factorio to the game list.
I heard about it, but haven’t played it. Can you describe what the programming element of it is?
I'd say the programming is implicit - you have to arrange a series of machines along a 2D grid to perform larger-scale tasks.
(OTOH I hope that the work I do for programmers might become part of the underlying infrastructure of tools for non-programmers.)

I'm not sure if it's "actual" programming or not, but when you start considering visual programming paradigms and whatnot, there's a lot of same stuff there. Compare to something like eg. Interesting. I see in some of the other examples that you record direct manipulation actions on tables into a script. I’ve been experimenting with that too. How does that work for your users? Do they feel it is more “authentic” to write code like the pros do?

Interesting. I see in some of the other examples that you record direct manipulation actions on tables into a script. I’ve been experimenting with that too. How does that work for your users? Do they feel it is more “authentic” to write code like the pros do?

our users are non programmers, some are sysadmins, some dbas, some managers, they use the manipulation to get the data to fit their needs, that's the part that gets the least complaints and observations (I guess that's a good thing?)

our users are non programmers, some are sysadmins, some dbas, some managers, they use the manipulation to get the data to fit their needs, that's the part that gets the least complaints and observations (I guess that's a good thing?)

the parts that they ask more about that are related to programming are asset management (how do I upload an image and get a reference to it) and what we call "dictionaries" (mappings from keys to values that they can use across visualizations and dashboards and can change in one place to reflect in all places. They care a lot about a thing being the same color (and a specific color) across all dashboards.

the parts that they ask more about that are related to programming are asset management (how do I upload an image and get a reference to it) and what we call "dictionaries" (mappings from keys to values that they can use across visualizations and dashboards and can change in one place to reflect in all places. They care a lot about a thing being the same color (and a specific color) across all dashboards.

After that they care a lot about adding interactivity (if the user clicks this, show that, filter this other thing, open another dashboard) this also brings the need to "override" configs (if user clicks a table row, open that dashboard but filter by this field in the row)

After that they care a lot about adding interactivity (if the user clicks this, show that, filter this other thing, open another dashboard) this also brings the need to "override" configs (if user clicks a table row, open that dashboard but filter by this field in the row)

a long way to say that they care a lot about "configuration management" we provide a "chain" of dictionaries and lookup, there are dictionaries at the user level, then per dashboard, then per visualization, if you lookup key k1 in dict d1, it will look first in the visualization, then the dashboard, then the user, so you can start global and then specialize if needed per dashboard or visualization

they also care about adding lightweight interactivity, react to clicks to filter, overlay info or open other dashboards and visualizations
they also care about adding lightweight interactivity, react to clicks to filter, overlay info or open other dashboards and visualizations

the programming part, the hardest is to undestand the iterator abstraction we use, I always go back and forth between "iterate by default a single item requrires explicit setup" and "single item by default, iteration requires explicit setup" (the one I'm using now)

I guess that's also "programming by example" :slightly_smiling_face:

attachments.title: Direct Programming
attachments.text:
To make programming more accessible to regular people it may help to make it less abstract and more concrete. Towards that end I am taking another run at the old ideas of macro recording and Programming by Example. Many such efforts abstracted user actions into conventional code. Instead I co-design the PL and UI to be analogous, so that programs and users have much the same capabilities, and programs look much like recorded transcripts of user actions. Conscientiously following this design principle leads in the opposite direction of current PL trends, towards a deeply imperative semantics and a de-emphasis of functional abstraction.

Any attempt to make programming more concrete faces two challenges: loops and conditionals, for they necessarily deal with hypothetical situations. My approach is to extend the direct manipulation metaphor onto _template_ states that prototype iteration, and _example_ states that witness alternative cases.

it was not meant as a critique, just a comparison to have some sort of discussion, I've heard research people say that papers are a long term discussion between different researchers, since I prefer demos (and it seems you too), I thought that a reply trying to show a different approach under the same structure and topics would be interesting, still thinking on how to structure long term discussions between projects of this sort, I like <@U5TCAFTD3> idea of having a fixed set of problems to solve with different tools and see what works, what doesn't, what's easy and what's hard, but my tool being a stream processing/aggregation thing is not well suited to implement a todo list :slightly_smiling_face:

the main difference is kind of fundamental, your tool understand types, so a collection has a type for the items, then you can show a "schema" of the item to operate on it as an explicit way to say "over all values of this shape", in my case since the inputs are schemaless the most I can do is infer the schema but I haven't done that yet, that's why I have the explicit iterator value, which is better than explicit imperative loops but next time I may go with "iteration by default, single item is a special case", something I've been exploring a little with the idea of an path item that represents a "column", that is, all items with that key in a colection, the column explicitly applies to all items and if used in a value context will work as a generator for that column (will generate a list of values for that field in the collection)

Thanks for sharing.  I was doing this sort of filter thing all day – both in Excel and Numbers.  Two observations...
Thanks for sharing.  I was doing this sort of filter thing all day – both in Excel and Numbers.  Two observations...
Small observation: you can assume an OR if someone drags two cells from the same column into the query.
Small observation: you can assume an OR if someone drags two cells from the same column into the query.

Big observation: I've played with some cool ideas with a notebook style for these kinds of operations.  It can work really well.  One view is to show data, operation, data, operation.  It's like a command-line transcript, but structured rather than text.  The other is more like a traditional GUI where you manipulate an object in place.  You can imagine this as stacking transformations.  Then switching between these views is just a matter of rotation.  From there you can go wild.  One natural path is to allow for branching and merging.  (In the context of tables, merging nodes often correspond to joins.)  If you aren't careful you find yourself in parametric modeling node-and-edges land.  I don't know if madness is the final destination, but at least you end up with a kind space of interaction modalities in which familiar conventions are projections.

Though I'm really oriented along a different axis.  It's not Programmers vs Non-programmers so much as... well it's not  Professionals vs Amateurs either, but that's getting closer.  I mean sometimes it's great to get an 80% solution quick.  Sometimes it's great to get a 99.9% solution through endless tweaking, blood, and tears.   So maybe there's another direction, call it up, that's neither quick nor twiddly.

i gather from the prior work of the founders that this is a server side technology, in that sense probably more like Dark language. The odd thing is that it is built in Clojure, but doesn't mention a language at all, so i infer that this project is about facilitating setting up server clusters, offering dashboards, scripting, failover control, etc. so more like an evolution of the tools AWS offers, but not proprietary to Amazon. This is of course a guess, but if you have been doing research for years, and haven't come up with a language, you probably didn't have the intention of creating one in the first place.

I don't consider a toolchain that doesn't include a language to be a major breakthrough. Without creating a new era of interchangeable parts, you haven't gone to the next gen IMHO

In educational circles they like to call this “computational thinking,” which is ill-defined but most closely captures the skill set I’m envisioning. One definition: the ability to understand, use, modify, and create _formal languages_ (programming languages, law, mathematics, games, …). Bonnie Nardi’s book “A Small Matter of Programming” does a good job explaining the universality of formal languages.

In educational circles they like to call this “computational thinking,” which is ill-defined but most closely captures the skill set I’m envisioning. One definition: the ability to understand, use, modify, and create _formal languages_ (programming languages, law, mathematics, games, …). Bonnie Nardi’s book “A Small Matter of Programming” does a good job explaining the universality of formal languages.

In the future, I think that computational thinking (CT) can be seen as a basic skill all people should have, just like standard literacy. Right now, our concept of CT is heavily tied to computers, which is bad because computers are counterintuitive and designed to work in accordance with the laws of physics, not the laws of human thought.

In the future, I think that computational thinking (CT) can be seen as a basic skill all people should have, just like standard literacy. Right now, our concept of CT is heavily tied to computers, which is bad because computers are counterintuitive and designed to work in accordance with the laws of physics, not the laws of human thought.

However, I think with sufficient understanding of humans and formal languages, we could develop an education around CT that teaches these skills in a way everyone can appreciate and benefit from, leading some people to be programmers, but more generally leading to a society where everyone can apply computational ideas to all walks of life. Crafts (see knitting languages), law (distinguishing precise from ambiguous language), whatever

However, I think with sufficient understanding of humans and formal languages, we could develop an education around CT that teaches these skills in a way everyone can appreciate and benefit from, leading some people to be programmers, but more generally leading to a society where everyone can apply computational ideas to all walks of life. Crafts (see knitting languages), law (distinguishing precise from ambiguous language), whatever
(so, in that sense, what I said wasn’t quite right--it’s not true that everyone should a programmer, but everyone should be a computational thinker!)
(so, in that sense, what I said wasn’t quite right--it’s not true that everyone should a programmer, but everyone should be a computational thinker!)

For example, I think the foundational ideas of precision (formal languages, syntax/grammar, semantics), abstraction (variables, functions), and modularity (interfaces, encapsulation, exchangeability) should be universal skills in the same way arithmetic, literacy, historical interpretation, etc. are.

For example, I think the foundational ideas of precision (formal languages, syntax/grammar, semantics), abstraction (variables, functions), and modularity (interfaces, encapsulation, exchangeability) should be universal skills in the same way arithmetic, literacy, historical interpretation, etc. are.

Java is the COBOL of our time. I hope they aren't trying to help Java continue its shambolic journey into the living graveyard of languages alongside COBOL and FORTRAN. Old languages never die, they get maintained indefinitely as their artifacts have business value, but very little new gets created in COBOL and FORTRAN today. This is one of many reasons i hate the TIOBE index, as it measures rotting dead carcasses by the pound as if that mattered to someone writing something today, and thus discriminates against super potent languages like ClojureScript and highly productive tools like Corona, or Adobe AIR, VB6 or Delphi. The better the tool, the shorter the program, and thus any non-verbose technology always looks like it is receding in market share when in fact less code is good!
